questions.question: What makes Uber's location ingest problem fundamentally different from a typical web API?
options: 5 million drivers each emit GPS updates at 4 Hz, producing ~20 million events per second — far beyond what a synchronous REST API can absorb.
Driver locations change very slowly, so the challenge is reducing storage cost rather than throughput.
GPS data is compressed, so the main challenge is decompression speed on the server side.
Each driver maintains a persistent WebSocket connection, making connection management the primary bottleneck.
questions.explanation: 20M events/sec is a stream ingest problem. REST APIs would collapse under that load; Kafka partitioned by driver ID is the correct tool.

questions.question: Why do hexagonal grids like H3 outperform square grids for proximity search?
options: In a hexagonal grid all 6 neighbors are equidistant from the center, whereas in a square grid diagonal neighbors are ~41% farther away than edge neighbors.
Hexagonal cells are smaller, so fewer drivers need to be scanned per lookup.
H3 cells support geofencing natively, while square grids do not.
Hexagonal grids require fewer bits to encode a cell ID than square grids at the same resolution.
questions.explanation: Equal distances to all neighbors means a k-ring search has uniform coverage and no edge-effect bias, giving more consistent proximity results.

questions.question: Uber's DISCO uses batch matching with a 500ms window in dense cities. What is the primary benefit over greedy nearest-driver matching?
options: Batch matching solves a bipartite assignment problem across all riders and drivers simultaneously, minimizing total system wait time rather than optimizing each match greedily.
Batch matching reduces the number of Kafka messages by grouping events into a single payload.
Greedy matching requires GPS data to be consistent before each match, which adds 500ms of lag anyway.
Batch matching avoids the need to compute ETAs because approximate distances are sufficient within a batch window.
questions.explanation: The Hungarian algorithm finds the globally optimal assignment. Greedy nearest may assign a close driver to a rider while a slightly farther driver would have reduced total system wait time.

questions.question: A Redis shard holding H3 cell membership data crashes. What is the immediate effect on Uber's dispatch system?
options: Drivers whose H3 cells map to the failed shard become invisible to the matching engine, causing a brief dispatch gap for those cells until the replica is promoted.
The entire dispatch system halts because driver availability cannot be determined for any cell.
Riders in all cells receive surge pricing until the shard recovers.
The matching engine falls back to querying the driver app directly via TChannel RPC.
questions.explanation: Only the cells on the failed shard are affected. Replica failover restores service; the impact is localized and time-bounded.

questions.question: What Uber-specific workflow system is used to make trip state machines durable across process failures?
options: Cadence (later open-sourced and rebranded as Temporal) — a workflow orchestration system that persists activity history so state is recoverable after any crash.
Ringpop — a consistent hashing ring that routes workflow events to the correct process.
TChannel — Uber's binary RPC protocol that retransmits workflow state on reconnect.
Docstore — Uber's document store that snapshots workflow state every 100ms.
questions.explanation: Cadence/Temporal records every workflow step event-sourced in a durable history, so a worker crash just replays the history on another worker.

questions.question: Surge pricing is calculated per H3 cell rather than per city. Why does this granularity matter?
options: Demand imbalances are hyper-local: one neighborhood may have a concert ending while another is quiet. Per-cell pricing sends drivers to the right block, not just the right city.
Per-city surge pricing requires a global lock that causes contention at 5M concurrent drivers.
H3 cells are the only geographic boundary recognized by local taxi regulations.
Per-cell pricing reduces fraud because surge zones are too small for coordinated manipulation.
questions.explanation: Granular surge creates granular driver incentives. A city-level multiplier might not move drivers to the specific neighborhood with shortage.

questions.question: A driver's GPS app stops sending updates. After 30 seconds, what should Uber's dispatch system do?
options: Remove the driver from all H3 cell membership sets (treat as OFFLINE) and re-dispatch any matched trip to another driver.
Keep the driver in cell sets but mark them as DEGRADED so the matching engine deprioritizes them.
Send a push notification to the driver and wait 5 minutes before taking any action.
Lock the driver's account until they manually reconnect through the driver app.
questions.explanation: A 30-second TTL on driver cell membership prevents stale drivers from blocking matches. Re-dispatch ensures the rider is not stranded.

questions.question: Which Uber-specific metric would you alert on to detect degraded ETA model performance?
options: ETA error rate: (|predicted_eta - actual_arrival_time| / actual_arrival_time) averaged over a rolling 5-minute window, alerting above 20%.
Kafka consumer lag exceeding 5 seconds, which indicates location data is stale.
Redis memory utilization exceeding 80%, which forces eviction of driver cell memberships.
Match rate dropping below 98%, which indicates the matching engine is overloaded.
questions.explanation: ETA error directly measures model quality and affects match scoring. Kafka lag and Redis memory are operational metrics, not ETA model health signals.
